For any choice of Gaussian surface.
solution:
According to Gauss law of electrostatics, the electric flux through an enclosed surface is given by ϕE=qencε0

From the formula of electric flux, ϕ=E→·ds→

Also, the relation between electric field and electric potential is given by V=E→·dr→

So, the formula E→=qencε0A is only applicable when the Gaussian surface is an equipotential surface and E→ is constant on the surface.

subject: Physics
solution:
By the property of electrostatic shielding in the conductors E=0 in the conductor. So, electric flux,=0 through a dotted Gaussian surface as shown



The net enclosed charge through the Gaussian surface=0,⇒ The net charge Q1 on the inner surface=0, but the equal and opposite induced charge on the surface will be distributed non uniformly on the inner surface

So, σ1=0

∵Q1=0 on the inner surface

So, net charge Q2=0 on the outer surface as conductor is neutral but ∵ outer surface is free from any electric field so no charge density exists on the outer surface. So, σ2=0 .

subject: Physics
options: Net potential of the system cannot be zero at a point but net electric field can be zero at that point
Net potential of the system at a point can be zero but net electric field can't be zero at that point
Both the net potential and the net field can be zero at a point
Both the net potential and the net electric field cannot be zero at a point
solution:
As we know, net potential will be algebraic sum of potential due to each charge, so we can write Vnet=∑kQiri.

As given in the question, Qi and ri are positive, so

∴  Vnet>0

But electric field is a vector quantity, so it can be zero for a given arrangement of charges. For example, electric field at the centre of the two equal charges at certain distance will be zero as electric field due to both charges will be in opposite direction and hence will cancel each other.

subject: Physics
solution:
Let us consider a spherical shell of radius $x$ and thickness $\mathrm{dx}$.
Charge on this shell $$ d q=\rho .4 \pi x^2 d x=\rho_0\left(1-\frac{x}{R}\right) \cdot 4 \pi x^2 d x $$ $\therefore$ Total charge in the spherical region from centre to $r(r < R)$ is $$ \begin{aligned} &q=\int d q=4 \pi \rho_0 \int_0^r\left(1-\frac{x}{R}\right) x^2 d x \\ &=4 \pi \rho_0\left[\frac{x^3}{3}-\frac{x^4}{4 R}\right]_0^r \\ &=4 \pi \rho_0\left[\frac{r^3}{3}-\frac{r^4}{4 R}\right] \\ &=4 \pi \rho_0 r^3\left[\frac{1}{3}-\frac{r}{4 R}\right] \end{aligned} $$ $\therefore$ Electric field at $\mathrm{r}, \mathrm{E}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{q}}{\mathrm{r}^2}$ $$ \begin{aligned} &=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{4 \pi \rho_0 r^3}{r^2}\left[\frac{1}{3}-\frac{r}{4 R}\right] \\ &=\frac{\rho_0}{\varepsilon_0}\left[\frac{r}{3}-\frac{r^2}{4 R}\right] \end{aligned} $$

subject: Physics
solution:
The potential is constant inside the sphere as the electric field is zero inside the sphere. So, the value is given by 

V=Q4πε0R for r<R

The potential at the surface is 

V=Q4πε0R

The potential outside the sphere starts decreasing and is given by the formula 

V=Q4πε0r for r>R

subject: Physics
solution:
Inside uniformly charged spherical shell, $E=0$
$\therefore \mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{III}$

For uniformly charged infinite plate
$E=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}$
$\therefore \mathrm{B} \rightarrow \mathrm{II}$

Outside of spherical shell
$E=\frac{Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r_2}=\frac{\sigma R^2}{\varepsilon_0 r^2}$
$\therefore \mathrm{C} \rightarrow \mathrm{IV}$

Between two plates $E=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_0}$
$\therefore \mathrm{D} \rightarrow \mathrm{I}$

subject: Physics
solution:
Using Coulomb's law the electrostatic force on A due to C

FAC=KqAqCrAC2=k×5×0.3×10-129×10-4=9×109×5×0.3×10-129×10-4

=1.5×10=15 N

Similarly, the force on A due to B

FAB=KqAqBrAB2=9×109×5×0.16×10-129×10-4=8 N

We can see that the forces are perpendicular to each other. Therefore, the net force experienced by charge at A is,

F=FAC2+FAB2=152+82=289=17 N

subject: Physics
solution:
According to Gauss's law, the electric flux through a closed surface is given by $\Phi = \dfrac{q_{\text{enclosed}}}{\varepsilon_0}$.

For the first sphere of radius $r_1 = 3$ cm centered at the origin, only the charge at $x = 2$ cm is enclosed.

$q_{\text{enclosed}, 1} = 8 \, \mu\text{C}$

$\Phi_1 = \dfrac{8 \, \mu\text{C}}{\varepsilon_0}$

For the second sphere of radius $r_2 = 5$ cm centered at the origin, both charges at $x = 2$ cm and $x = 4$ cm are enclosed.

$q_{\text{enclosed}, 2} = 8 \, \mu\text{C} - 2 \, \mu\text{C} = 6 \, \mu\text{C}$

$\Phi_2 = \dfrac{6 \, \mu\text{C}}{\varepsilon_0}$

The ratio of the electric flux is:

$\dfrac{\Phi_1}{\Phi_2} = \dfrac{8}{6} = \dfrac{4}{3}$

Answer: $4 : 3$

subject: Physics
solution:
Gauss's law states that the net flux of an electric field in a closed surface is directly proportional to the enclosed electric charge. Therefore,

ϕsmaller cube =2Qε0

Since the smaller cube is inside the bigger cube, so total charge contained by the bigger cube will be 2Q+3Q=5Q.

ϕbigger cube =5Qε0

Therefore, required ratio will be, 

ϕsmaller cube ϕbigger cube =25.

solution:
Electric field due to charged sheet for charge density +σ is E→=+σ2∈0 and for -σ is E→=-σ2∈0.

Assuming RHS to be n^

E→I=σ2∈0-n^+σ2∈0-n^=-σ∈0n^  (leftwards)

E→II=σ2∈0-n^+σ2∈0n^=0

E→III=σ2∈0n^+σ2∈0n^=σ∈0n^    (rightwards)
